data.title: Mercedes, Nvidia, and Uber to partner on large-scale commercial robotaxi deployment
data.description:
The robotaxi roster of players expanded even further on Thursday after Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF), Nvidia (NVDA), and Uber Technologies (UBER) announced a three-way collaboration to build a global robotaxi platform that will use Mercedes' (MGAF) new S-Class, Nvidia's (NVDA) autonomous driving hardware and software stack, and Uber's (UBER) ride-hailing network to offer driverless rides in major markets. 

The robotaxi service will run on Mercedes-Benz's (MBGAF) MB.OS operating system, allowing Nvidia (NVDA) and other partners to layer high-level automated driving applications on top while the German automaker keeps control of core vehicle software and integration. 

For its part, Nvidia (NVDA) will supply its DRIVE Hyperion architecture, Alpamayo AI driving models, and simulation and safety tools to deliver level 4-capable autonomy, building on prior announcements that its AV stack would reach the road with Mercedes and feed into Uber’s plan to deploy large autonomous fleets later this decade. Uber's (UBER) role is to simply fold the robotaxis into its existing platform so human-driven vehicles and autonomous S-Class robotaxis operate side by side.

While the companies did not provide a launch timeline, the collaboration was called a step toward large-scale commercial deployments in multiple global cities. "Together, Mercedes-Benz, Nvidia, and Uber will build a global platform that makes autonomous driving available to everyone," stated Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang in a video shown at a Mercedes event.

data.title: Parker-Hannifin shares jump on earnings beat, higher 2026 outlook
data.description:
Shares of Parker-Hannifin (PH) rose as much as 5.3% Thursday, outperforming a broader market selloff, after the industrial and aerospace supplier reported stronger-than-expected quarterly earnings and revenue, and raised its full-year outlook.

Adjusted earnings of $7.65 a share topped the Wall Street consensus estimate of $7.19, while revenue rose 9% to $5.2 billion, above analysts’ expectations of $5.07 billion.

Net income fell to $845 million, or $6.60 a share, from $949 million, or $8.30 a share, a year earlier, when results included a one-time after-tax gain related to divestitures. On an adjusted basis, net income increased 15% to $980 million.

Margins and cash performance

Segment operating margin expanded to 23.9%, up 180 basis points, while adjusted segment operating margin increased to 27.1%, reflecting improved operating leverage across the company’s industrial and aerospace businesses.

The company reported higher profitability despite the absence of prior-year divestiture gains, supported by growth in aerospace and defense, in-plant and industrial and off-highway markets.

“This was another outstanding quarter that reflected the performance of our global team and the strength of our transformed portfolio,” Parker-Hannifin (PH) Chair and Chief Executive Officer Jenny Parmentier said in a statement, adding that results were supported by strong aerospace demand and signs of recovery in industrial markets.

Aerospace strength and order growth

Sales in the Aerospace Systems segment climbed 14.5%, driven by higher commercial original equipment manufacturer output and aftermarket demand. Aerospace backlog rose to a record $8 billion, while total company backlog increased to $11.7 billion.

Companywide order rates rose 9%, with all reported businesses posting positive growth during the quarter.

Raised 2026 guidance

Parker-Hannifin (PH) raised its outlook for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2026, citing sustained aerospace demand and improving industrial conditions.

The company now expects reported sales growth of 5.5% to 7.5%, segment operating margin of 23.7% to 24.1%, or 27.0% to 27.4% on an adjusted basis, and adjusted earnings of $30.40 to $31.00 a share, up from its prior forecast.

data.title: Hexcel outlines $2.0B–$2.1B 2026 sales target amid commercial aerospace ramp and margin expansion focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hexcel Corporation (HXL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Gentile indicated strong confidence in a sustained ramp-up of commercial aircraft production rates, citing a commercial aircraft backlog exceeding 17,000 and a delivery shortfall of at least 5,300 aircraft. Gentile stated that "even with this historically high backlog, airlines are still ordering new aircraft," highlighting the robust demand for lightweight, fuel-efficient planes incorporating Hexcel's materials.
  • Gentile noted positive catalysts across major programs: "Across all our major programs, the A350, the 320, 787 and the 737, we see positive catalysts that a sustained recovery and ramp-up in commercial aircraft build rates is beginning to take hold." He pointed to in-house A350 production for Airbus, LEAP engine build increases, new assembly lines, and Boeing’s output expansion as supporting these trends.
  • Gentile emphasized the company’s sole-source positions and long-term contracts: "When Airbus and Boeing achieve publicly disclosed peak build rates, we expect to generate $500 million in incremental sales annually from those sole-source contracts."
  • Strategic actions included the launch of a $350 million accelerated share repurchase (ASR) program, a 6% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.18 per share, facility rationalizations in Austria, Belgium, and the UK, and leadership changes, including the appointment of Mike Lenz as Interim CFO.
  • CFO Michael Lenz stated, "We closed the year with a strong fourth quarter and a return to year-over-year growth. The higher sales supported adjusted operating margin expansion, illustrating the operating leverage opportunity ahead."

Outlook

  • Gentile projected 2026 sales in the range of $2.0 billion to $2.1 billion, adjusted EPS between $2.10 and $2.30, and free cash flow greater than $195 million. He said, "We expect to generate in excess of $1 billion in free cash flow cumulatively over the next 4 years from 2026 to 2029."
  • Commercial aerospace growth for 2026 is expected to be in the "low to mid-double digits," while defense is forecasted for "low to mid-single digits growth."
  • The 2026 plan assumes deliveries for major programs: about 80 A350 units (up from 57 in 2025), "low to mid-700s" for A320, "mid-400s" for the 737, and "90 to 100" for the 787.
  • Lenz noted that guidance incorporates the absence of sales from divested and refocused facilities and that foreign exchange will be a headwind in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Hexcel reported full year 2025 sales of $1.894 billion, adjusted EPS of $1.76, and free cash flow of $157 million. Fourth quarter sales were $492 million, up 3.7% year over year.
  • Commercial aerospace sales in Q4 reached $299.5 million, up 7.6% year over year, while defense, space and other segment sales were $191.8 million, down 1.9% due to the Austrian business divestment.
  • Lenz reported gross margin of 24.6% for Q4, down from 25% in the prior year, and Q4 adjusted operating income of $65 million (13.3% of sales). He cited "strong cost control" and lower SG&A, with operating expenses at 11.4% of sales, down from 13%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 was $346 million. Leverage stood at just under 2.7x net debt to adjusted EBITDA at year-end, with a stated intent to return to the 1.5 to 2x range in 2026.

Q&A

  • Kenneth Herbert, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about the revenue guidance breakdown and A350 assumptions. Gentile explained the commercial aerospace growth rates and detailed the 80-unit A350 production plan, noting, "the purchase orders are very strong this year... we've got good visibility on the purchase orders — firm purchase orders all the way out through May."
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: Sought quantification of one-time items and incremental margin upside. Gentile stated, "mid-30s is what we're seeing based on the current plan" for incremental margins, with higher production rates as the key upside.
  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: Queried about fixed vs. variable costs and hiring. Gentile described ongoing cost discipline, stating headcount finished 2025 about 330 positions lower than 2024 and selective hiring planned for 2026.
  • John McNulty, BMO: Asked about incremental margins and capital returns. Gentile affirmed operating leverage would drive margins, and further buybacks could be considered after deleveraging.
  • Michael Ciarmoli, Truist: Requested revenue guidance by end market and price/cost updates. Gentile gave commercial and defense growth figures and described hedging practices for raw materials.
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: Inquired about composite margin drivers. Lenz asserted, "there was nothing specifically unique" in Q4 margins, attributing strength to cost control and lower incentive compensation payouts.
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Asked about risks to profitability and FX headwinds. Lenz explained, "the weakening of the dollar to the euro... shifted the effect of foreign exchange on earnings from a tailwind... to a headwind in Q3 and into Q4."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for detail on margin sustainability, hiring, production assumptions, and cash deployment, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as commercial recovery and order trends improved.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, highlighting recovery and operational discipline. In Q&A, Gentile repeatedly referenced "confidence" and described assumptions as "conservative," but was candid about headwinds and cost risks.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone showed increased optimism, with management now highlighting tangible order improvements and a clearer path to production recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with sales and EPS targets explicitly outlined, compared to the prior quarter’s more cautious commentary.
  • Management now reports that most OEM destocking is "generally behind us," versus prior caution about lingering inventory overhang.
  • The outlook for commercial aerospace has shifted upward, with actual order activity and backlog providing a firmer basis for growth assumptions.
  • The tone from management has turned more confident, especially regarding production ramp and margin expansion, while analysts’ skepticism has lessened but remains focused on execution risks and cost controls.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited lingering destocking as a "watch item," ongoing FX headwinds, and potential supply chain disruptions as ongoing risks.
  • Facility closures and divestments were executed to streamline operations, but management committed to monitoring impacts on sales and margins.
  • Analysts raised questions on incremental costs, FX exposure, and the sustainability of margin improvements.

Final Takeaway

Hexcel management emphasized that as major commercial aircraft programs ramp up, the company is well positioned for growth, with incremental sales and operating leverage expected to drive margin expansion. The company projects a return to pre-pandemic production levels in 2026 and laid out clear financial and operational targets, supported by strong commercial order trends and cost discipline. Leadership transitions and portfolio rationalizations are designed to support these objectives, with a focus on capturing the upside as industry demand accelerates.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tal Education signals moderation in growth rate for H2 2026 amid strong AI-driven device engagement
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TAL Education Group (TAL) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Zhuangzhuang Peng, President & CFO, stated the company "continue[s] to make steady progress on our strategic priorities with a consistent focus on supporting the holistic development of our students." He highlighted ongoing innovation and user engagement across both online and offline learning services, while noting, "changes in market demand and advances in technology continue to introduce new dynamics." Peng confirmed the company faces intense competition in learning devices, content, hardware, and AI, and will "advance our strategic initiatives and flexibly allocate resources to build long-term capabilities." He explained, "we may face occasional variability and limited visibility in our financial performance due to seasonal demand shifts, competitive pressures and deliberate resource reallocation."
  • Peng reported, "our learning services recorded year-over-year revenue growth across both off-line Peiyou programs and online enrichment offerings."
  • The company expanded its offline Peiyou network and introduced new technology-enhanced programs, including immersive classroom solutions and technology themes such as 3D printing. Peng described, "We also expanded our offerings to include more technology themes such as 3D printing with the goal of fostering interest in emerging technologies."
  • For learning devices, Peng emphasized the integration of "over 2 decades of educational insights into the interaction logic of our learning devices," focusing on AI tutoring capabilities. He stated, "Our goal is to steadily evolve our learning devices into personalized AI companion that inspires thinking and support deeper learning."
  • At CES 2026, TAL showcased the "AI Buddy," a smart companion for children that received the CES Picks Award.
  • Peng reported, "our net revenues were USD 770.2 million... representing year-over-year increases of 27.0%." He further stated, "our non-GAAP income from operations and non-GAAP net income attributable to TAL for the quarter were USD 104.0 million and USD 141.4 million, respectively."
  • Jackson Ding, Deputy CFO, highlighted, "Peiyou small class enrichment programs demonstrated stable operations, delivering year-over-year growth driven by increased enrollment." He affirmed, "Operationally, our learning devices delivered year-over-year growth in both revenue and sales volume this quarter. The average weekly active rate among learning device users remained at approximately 80%."

Outlook

  • Peng described technology integration as a long-term strategic priority, aiming to "continue enhancing our product design and service delivery across our businesses."
  • The company is "implementing more agile channel management strategies" for newer businesses like learning devices, and is "reinforcing our multichannel ecosystem." Peng reiterated, "improving overall profitability remains a key priority for us," but short-term variability is expected due to "market conditions, investment cycles and seasonal fluctuations."
  • Management expects continued investment in capabilities and a focus on "long-term sustainable development rather than short-term financial outcomes."

Financial Results

  • Net revenues were reported at $770.2 million, a year-over-year increase of 27.0%.
  • Cost of revenues increased by 18.0% to $338.4 million.
  • Gross profit reached $431.8 million, up 35.0% year-over-year. Gross margin increased to 56.1% from 52.7% the previous year.
  • Selling and marketing expenses decreased by 2.8% to $220.1 million; non-GAAP selling and marketing expenses as a percentage of net revenues dropped from 36.7% to 28.3%.
  • General and administrative expenses rose by 7.1% to $118.6 million, while non-GAAP G&A as a percentage of revenue decreased to 14.4%.
  • Income from operations was $93.1 million, compared to a loss from operations of ($17.4 million) in the prior year period.
  • Net income attributable to TAL was $130.6 million, compared to $23.1 million the previous year.
  • Net cash provided by operating activities was $526.7 million.
  • The company repurchased 844,856 common shares for $27.7 million during the recent period under its $600 million share repurchase program.

Q&A

  • Felix Liu, UBS: Questioned the expansion strategy and growth outlook for the Peiyou network. Zhuangzhuang Peng responded, "we really maintain our disciplined operational approach to network expansion...based on this measured and multidimensional approach, the business remains on a stable growth trajectory." He noted, "revenue growth during the quarter was primarily driven by increased enrollment, while our ASP remained relatively stable," and cautioned, "we anticipate a gradual moderation in the pace of revenue growth in FY 2026."
  • Charlotte Wei, HSBC: Asked about the slowdown in top line momentum and growth outlook for learning devices. Jackson Ding explained, "the moderation in our top line growth was primarily driven by a deceleration in the growth rate of our learning device business," noting product launch timing and a higher comparison base. He added, "we expect continued fluctuation in learning device revenue," and the group growth rate "to moderate in the second half of this fiscal year."
  • Liping Zhao, CICC: Requested details on learning device sales and market competition. Peng stated, "we saw year-over-year volume growth driven by enhancements to our product portfolio and channel strategies," but the business "reported an adjusted operating loss as it remains in an investment phase." He described the market as "dynamic," with AI "fundamentally transforming the educational technology landscape."
  • Timothy Zhao, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about profitability drivers and margin outlook. Ding responded, "the improvement primarily reflects the volatility in our selling and marketing expenses, coupled with disciplined cost management... We would caution against using this quarter's margin performance as a benchmark for future periods." He reiterated, "the breakeven time line remains uncertain" for learning devices.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive to slightly cautious tone, pressing for clarity on growth moderation and profitability, while congratulating management on strong results.
  • Management's tone was disciplined and cautiously optimistic in prepared remarks, but slightly more guarded in Q&A when discussing margin sustainability and growth moderation, with Peng noting, "we anticipate a gradual moderation in the pace of revenue growth in FY 2026."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' questions shifted from competitive landscape and expansion to a stronger focus on slowing growth rates and profitability. Management continues to project confidence in long-term strategy but underscores near-term variability and investment priorities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a pronounced slowdown in revenue growth rate compared to Q2, highlighted by management as a result of timing in product launches and higher comparison bases.
  • Gross margin improved to 56.1% from 57.0% in Q2, while selling and marketing expenses were notably lower this quarter.
  • Management's guidance language shifted from emphasizing expansion to a greater focus on sustainable growth, margin discipline, and managing short-term fluctuations.
  • Analysts in the current call pressed more on future growth rates and margin outlook versus the previous quarter's focus on market expansion and product launches.
  • The learning device business remains in an investment phase, with operating losses persisting, in contrast to more optimistic profit discussions in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged "seasonal demand shifts, competitive pressures and deliberate resource reallocation" as sources of financial performance variability.
  • The learning device business operates in a "highly competitive environment" and continues to report operating losses as it invests in product innovation and channel expansion.
  • Short-term fluctuations are expected in both revenue and margins due to shifts in market conditions and product cycles.
  • Analysts expressed concern over moderating growth rates and margin sustainability.

Final Takeaway

TAL Education Group's third quarter results underscore a strategic pivot from rapid expansion to disciplined growth and profitability, with the company highlighting strong engagement in AI-driven learning devices and stable user demand across core offerings. While competition and market dynamics are expected to drive near-term variability—particularly in the learning device segment—management remains focused on long-term capability building, product innovation, and prudent resource allocation to ensure sustainable development.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CSW Industrials signals margin expansion and synergy gains following $1B acquisition spree
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CSW Industrials (CSW) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Armes reported that "our team delivered record fiscal third quarter results in both revenue and adjusted EBITDA, despite market headwinds and economic uncertainty that has been present for most of this fiscal year and which has been most pronounced in the residential HVAC/R end market."
  • Armes highlighted that the company completed three acquisitions in the quarter, including the $650 million purchase of MARS Parts, marking CSW's largest acquisition to date, and the acquisitions of Hydrotex Holdings and ProAction Fluids for $26.5 million in total. Over the past 12 months, four synergistic transactions totaling approximately $1 billion were executed.
  • Armes noted, "We've invested $70 million in open market share repurchases during the quarter, emphasizing our dedication to maximizing shareholder returns."
  • CFO James Perry stated, "During the third fiscal quarter of 2026, we delivered a record revenue of $233 million, up 20% as compared to the prior year, driven primarily by our acquisitions over the last year." He added, "Adjusted consolidated EBITDA grew 7%. Adjusted EPS for the fiscal third quarter was $1.42, demonstrating resilience amid challenging market conditions. Recognizing that this reflects a 21% reduction compared to the same period last year, the reduction in adjusted EPS was primarily driven by $10 million of higher interest expense."
  • Perry also explained that "we now expect to exceed this initial objective" of $10 million in run rate synergies from the MARS Parts acquisition and to reach a 30% EBITDA margin for this business within 12 months.

Outlook

  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone regarding emerging order patterns and inventory normalization, with Armes stating, "We confidently maintain our expectations to achieve our operational and financial goals for this acquisition."
  • The company reiterated the long-term expectation of mid- to high single-digit organic growth in the Contractor Solutions segment but refrained from issuing specific guidance for fiscal 2027, stating, "While it is too early in the season to forecast what we expect in calendar 2026 and for our fiscal 2027, we are cautiously optimistic and encouraged by order patterns starting to emerge."

Financial Results

  • CSW Industrials posted record quarterly revenue of $233 million, a 20% increase driven by acquisitions, offset by a 2.9% reduction in consolidated organic revenue mainly in Contractor Solutions.
  • Adjusted consolidated EBITDA reached $45 million, up 7% year-over-year, with a margin of 19.2%, down 250 basis points due to acquisition-related margin dilution and higher input costs.
  • Adjusted EPS was $1.42, down 21% from the prior year, impacted by increased interest expense and higher operating expenses from acquired businesses.
  • Contractor Solutions revenue was $168 million, with 27% growth, but organic revenue declined 5.1% due to ongoing destocking in the residential HVAC/R market.
  • Specialized Reliability Solutions revenue increased 10.8% to $38 million, while Engineered Building Solutions revenue decreased 1% to $28.5 million.
  • Cash flow from operations was $28.9 million, and free cash flow was $22.7 million for the quarter.
  • The company ended the quarter with a net debt-to-EBITDA leverage ratio of 2.3x, following the funding of acquisitions and share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about order improvements and organic growth trends. Perry responded that order rates in January were "very good" and customer inventories were becoming balanced, but it was too early to quantify organic growth rates.
  • Charles Brown for Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs: Asked about confidence in organic growth despite a weak housing market. Perry stated that while it's "a little early," the company expects to return to mid- to high single-digit organic growth over time, with easier comps ahead.
  • Brown inquired about pricing strategies amid tariffs. Perry said, "We have never been shy about taking price increases and pushing those through if we see the costs moving up and we're very transparent with that with our customers."
  • Brown also asked about capital allocation priorities and future M&A. Perry explained that with a leverage ratio at 2.3x, "that leverage ratio... moved up from the acquisitions, but we also repurchased $70 million of stock in the quarter... We're very comfortable at 2.3." Armes added that integration of recent acquisitions is going well and that the period of digestion would be "quarters, not years."
  • Natalia Bak, Citi: Asked about weather impacts on replacement activity and cost synergies from acquisitions. Perry said no significant impact from cold weather was seen, and that synergy realization is ahead of plan but in early stages.
  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Queried about one-off costs and margin recovery. Perry clarified that most transaction expenses are behind, with some integration costs remaining, and a specific inventory write-off was onetime in nature.
  • Sano also asked about Engineered Building Solutions' outlook. Armes described it as the "most cyclical business" but noted opportunities for organic growth through new product development and positive market reception.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, probing on organic growth normalization, pricing power, and integration synergies, while seeking clarity on margin trajectory and capital allocation.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks, but acknowledged seasonality and market headwinds, using phrases like "cautiously optimistic" and "very pleased with what we're seeing so far." In Q&A, responses were detailed and transparent, yet careful not to provide premature projections.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted to more caution around organic growth, but showed increased confidence in integration progress and synergy attainment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained cautious, with no new forward projections, but order trends and synergy expectations were more positive than last quarter.
  • Strategic focus shifted from anticipation of M&A closing to integration and synergy realization, with management emphasizing digestion and debt paydown over new large-scale acquisitions.
  • Analysts continued to focus on organic growth, pricing, and capital allocation, but questions on M&A pipeline and synergy capture were more prominent this quarter.
  • Key metrics such as revenue and EBITDA grew year-over-year, but adjusted EPS declined due to higher interest costs and acquisition-related expenses.
  • Management’s confidence in achieving synergy targets for acquisitions increased, while their outlook on end-market demand remained reserved.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing destocking in the residential HVAC/R market and seasonality as factors affecting organic revenue.
  • Margin compression in acquired businesses and higher input costs, particularly from tariffs, were highlighted as challenges.
  • Perry noted that restructuring actions in Specialized Reliability Solutions are expected to improve margins, with benefits starting in April.
  • Analysts raised concerns on the timing of organic growth normalization, pricing flexibility, and potential impact of weather or further market weakness.

Final Takeaway

CSW Industrials delivered record third quarter revenue and adjusted EBITDA, driven by recent acquisitions and supported by disciplined capital allocation. Integration of new businesses, particularly MARS Parts, is progressing ahead of initial synergy targets, and management expressed confidence in achieving operational and financial goals. While organic growth remains subdued due to end market headwinds, order rates and inventory normalization provide cautious optimism for improvement, positioning the company for continued value creation as acquisition synergies are realized and debt is reduced in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Valero outlines $1.7B capital investment plan for 2026 while advancing optimization projects
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valero Energy Corporation (VLO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Lane Riggs, CEO, President & Chairman, opened by noting "our continued commitment to safe, reliable and environmentally responsible operations resulted in record refining throughput and record ethanol production for both the fourth quarter and the full year." Riggs emphasized a record for mechanical availability in 2025, attributing these achievements to "the hard work, expertise and dedication of our entire team."

  • Riggs highlighted strong financial results, stating "we captured favorable refining margins during the quarter driven by strong product cracks and widening sour crude discounts and our fourth quarter performance capped off excellent financial results for the year."

  • Riggs announced continued progress on the $230 million SEC unit optimization project at the St. Charles refinery, expected to begin operations in the second half of 2026. He added that refining fundamentals are expected to remain supported by "continued demand growth and tight supply environment driven by limited capacity additions" and cited the benefit of increased Canadian and Venezuelan crude supply.

  • On strategic direction, Riggs reiterated the company's commitment to "our disciplined capital allocation framework that prioritizes balance sheet strength, disciplined capital investments and shareholder returns."

  • CFO Homer Bhullar reported "net income attributable to Valero stockholders was $1.1 billion or $3.73 per share" for Q4 2025, compared to $281 million or $0.88 per share for Q4 2024. Adjusted net income was $1.2 billion or $3.82 per share for Q4 2025. Bhullar also noted, "the refining segment reported $1.7 billion of operating income for the fourth quarter of 2025."

  • Bhullar detailed, "refining throughput volumes in the fourth quarter of 2025 averaged 3.1 million barrels per day or 98% throughput capacity utilization," and described record throughput for both the quarter and the full year.

  • Bhullar confirmed a 6% increase to the quarterly cash dividend and described a payout ratio of 66% for Q4 2025, with $1.4 billion in shareholder cash returns for the quarter. He added, "we ended the year with 299 million shares outstanding, reflecting a reduction of 5% for the year."

Outlook

  • Valero expects capital investments attributable to the company for 2026 to be approximately $1.7 billion, with about $1.4 billion allocated to sustaining the business and the rest to growth projects. Management stated, "these growth projects are focused primarily on shorter cycle optimization investments that enhance crude and product optionality across our refining system as well as efficiency and rate expansion projects within our ethanol plants."

  • First quarter 2026 guidance includes Gulf Coast refining throughput volumes between 1.695 million and 1.745 million barrels per day, Mid-Continent at 430,000 to 450,000 barrels per day, West Coast at 160,000 to 180,000 barrels per day, and North Atlantic at 485,000 to 505,000 barrels per day.

  • Guidance for Q1 2026: refining cash operating expenses expected at $5.17 per barrel, renewable diesel sales volumes at approximately 260 million gallons, and ethanol production at 4.6 million gallons per day.

Financial Results

  • Valero reported net income attributable to stockholders of $1.1 billion or $3.73 per share for Q4 2025. Adjusted net income was $1.2 billion or $3.82 per share. The refining segment produced $1.7 billion of operating income.

  • Refining throughput volumes reached 3.1 million barrels per day, or 98% utilization, marking a record for both the quarter and full year.

  • Renewable Diesel segment operating income was $92 million for Q4 2025, with segment sales volumes averaging 3.1 million gallons per day. Ethanol segment operating income was $117 million, with record production volumes of 4.8 million gallons per day.

  • Capital investments for Q4 2025 totaled $412 million. Shareholder cash returns were $1.4 billion for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Theresa Chen, Barclays: Asked about supply-demand dynamics for light products and crack spreads, as well as the impact of Venezuelan crude. Gary Simmons, COO, noted a "significant build in total light product inventory" but attributed this to high refinery utilization, not weak demand, and expects "demand is outpacing additional supply."

  • Chen further inquired about the absorption of Venezuelan crude. Randy Hawkins stated that Valero has historically run as much as 240,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan heavy and expects increased processing capability now. Hawkins observed, "all looks pretty favorable. I think, for discounts kind of heading into 2026."

  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Asked about return of capital and share repurchases. Bhullar responded, "absent those uses of cash, we're going to continue to lean into share repurchases... our return on buybacks is above mid-teens over that 10-year period."

  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Questioned improved cash flow generation. Bhullar attributed it to disciplined capital investments, strong balance sheet, and share repurchases. Gupta asked about renewable diesel earnings outlook; Eric Fisher explained, "there's a lot of capacity off-line... I think you're going to see 2026 starting off more like the second half of '25, and so that would indicate a stronger year in '26 versus '25."

  • Additional questions covered coker utilization, labor contracts, West Coast refining, Benicia refinery shutdown, and ethanol segment drivers. Management provided details on each, with Richard Walsh confirming the phased shutdown at Benicia and continued supply commitments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed curiosity and cautious optimism, probing on inventory dynamics, crude slate flexibility, capital returns, renewable fuels policy, and operational efficiency. Some skepticism appeared regarding the sustainability of high cash flows and renewable diesel margins.

  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing operational achievements, disciplined capital allocation, and the ability to adapt to market shifts. Trigger phrases included "we remain committed" and "we are poised to capture."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained confident but fielded more granular questions related to heavy crude, renewable diesel policy, and operational optimization. Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, probing for sustainability and future performance drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital investments decreased from $1.9 billion in 2025 to $1.7 billion for 2026, with a shift to shorter-cycle optimization and efficiency projects.

  • Refining throughput volumes and mechanical availability achieved records in both quarters, with Q4 seeing higher utilization (98% vs. 97%).

  • Q4 marked a rebound in Renewable Diesel segment income ($92 million) after a Q3 loss ($28 million). Ethanol segment income increased quarter-over-quarter.

  • The company completed the transition to a new CFO and continued share count reduction.

  • Analysts’ questions shifted from broader macro themes and AI/automation in Q3 to more granular questions on crude slate optimization, renewable policy, and segment profitability in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the need for final policy guidance on renewable diesel credits, ongoing regulatory uncertainty, and the impact of tariffs and Supreme Court decisions on renewables.

  • Analysts raised concerns about inventory builds, supply-demand imbalances, sustainability of cash flows, and the timeline and operational impact of the Benicia refinery shutdown.

  • Management cited continued discipline on capital allocation, operational flexibility, and the ability to adjust crude slates as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Valero’s fourth quarter showcased record operational performance, robust financial results, and a disciplined commitment to shareholder returns. Management is advancing targeted optimization projects and expects refining fundamentals to remain strong, while closely monitoring policy clarity in renewables and strategically managing capital investments for 2026. The company’s focus remains on operational excellence, efficient capital deployment, and maintaining flexibility in a dynamic market environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Activist Irenic pushing SPS Commerce to explore sale - report
data.description:
Activist investor Irenic Capital Management has taken a stake in SPS Commerce (SPSC) and wants the supply chain software maker to explore its options, including a sale.

The activist has engaged with the board and management, according to a Bloomberg report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter. 

“We regularly engage with our investors and thoughtfully evaluate all input that advances our shared goal of creating sustainable long-term shareholder value,” the SPS Commerce spokesperson told Bloomberg.

The Irenic push comes after fellow activist Anson Funds Management last month disclosed a stake in SPS and wants the company to oust its CEO and put itself up for sale.

Shares of SPS fell 2% on Thursday. The stock has dropped more than 50% over the past year.

data.title: Nubank gets OCC approval for U.S. bank charter
data.description:
Brazilian digital bank Nubank (NU) has won approval from the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency to establish a de novo U.S. bank, it said on Thursday.

The company applied in September for the national bank charter, seeking to operate under a comprehensive federal framework and facilitate the launch of deposit accounts, credit cards, lending and digital asset custody.

With the approval, Nu (NU) is now in the bank organization phase, in which Nu satisfies certain OCC conditions alongside pending required approvals from the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. and the Federal Reserve. During this phase, the company will focus on fully capitalizing the institution within 12 months and opening the bank within 18 months.

“This approval isn't just an expansion of our operation; it’s an opportunity to prove our thesis that a digital-first, customer-centric model is the future of financial services globally," Nu Holdings (NU) Founder and CEO David Vélez said in a statement. "While we remain fully focused on our core markets in Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia, this step allows us to build the next generation of banking in the United States," he added.

The new Nubank, N.A., unit will be led by N (NU) Co-Founder Cristina Junqueira, who has relocated to the U.S. Roberto Campos Neto, former president of the Central Bank of Brazil, will serve as chairman of the board.

NU shares slipped 0.8% in midafternoon trading.

data.title: Royal Caribbean expects double-digit revenue growth and $17.70-$18.10 EPS in 2026 while expanding river cruise fleet
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jason Liberty stated that "2025 was an outstanding year, defined by strong demand for our brands and vacation experiences, disciplined execution of our strategies, strong balance sheet management and robust financial performance." He highlighted delivery of a record 9.4 million vacations, nearly $18 billion in total revenue, and 33% earnings growth for the year.
  • Liberty announced an expansion of Celebrity River Cruises with a commitment for 10 additional ships, aiming for 20 vessels by 2031, positioning Celebrity as one of the largest European river cruise operators. He also revealed the launch of the Royal Caribbean brand's new Discovery Class ships with two firm orders and options for four more.
  • Strategic investments were emphasized, including a "next evolution" of the loyalty program, Points Choice, which allows earning and applying points across all three vacation brands.
  • Liberty reported that "Fourth quarter net yields grew 2.5% and adjusted EPS was $2.80, higher than our guidance." He added, "We generated nearly $6.5 billion of operating cash flow and returned $2 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks."
  • The CEO stressed technology and AI as foundational advantages, noting a "25% year-over-year increase in active users on the app in the fourth quarter," with e-commerce traffic up 10% year-over-year.
  • CFO Naftali Holtz said, "Net yields grew 2.5% in constant currency, 5 basis points above the midpoint of our guidance," and described total revenue growth of 13% in the fourth quarter. Holtz added, "Net cruise costs, excluding fuel, decreased 6.3% in constant currency, in line with our guidance." For the full year, Holtz reported, "Adjusted EBITDA grew by 17.6% to just over $7 billion and adjusted EPS grew 33% to $15.64."

Outlook

  • Liberty projected that revenue is expected to "increase double digit year-over-year, resulting in full year net yield growth in the range of 1.5% to 3.5%." Full year adjusted earnings per share is expected to be between $17.70 and $18.10, representing a 14% year-over-year increase.
  • Holtz indicated capacity is expected to be up 6.7% year-over-year in 2026. Caribbean yields have grown 35% since 2019, with continued yield growth expected in 2026. Europe will account for 15% of capacity, growing 5% versus last year.
  • Net cruise costs, excluding fuel, are projected to be flat to up 1% for 2026. Full year fuel expense is anticipated at approximately $1.17 billion. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be just under $8 billion with margin just over 40%.
  • For Q1 2026, adjusted EPS guidance is $3.18 to $3.28.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter total revenue increased by 13%, with net yields up 2.5% in constant currency.
  • Full year total revenue rose 8.8%, adjusted EBITDA surpassed $7 billion, and operating cash flow reached $6.4 billion.
  • The company returned $2 billion to shareholders and invested more than $5 billion in future growth. Liquidity at quarter end was $7.2 billion and leverage was below 3x.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about momentum into 2026 and market share opportunity. Liberty responded that "our business is growing," with capacity up 6.7%, and demand accelerating, especially among loyalists. He said, "we're building more trust, and we're also enhancing the overall guest experience."
  • Steven Wieczynski, Stifel: Inquired about Caribbean capacity and yield guidance. Liberty said "we continue to see strong demand for the Caribbean, and we continue to see strong demand for our broader organization." On yield guidance, he noted that "typically, we think about moderate yield growth somewhere between 2% to 4%."
  • James Hardiman, Citi: Asked about organic vs. inorganic growth and the impact of new ships and destinations. Liberty indicated about half of yield growth comes from new hardware, and the rest from like-for-like growth. Michael Bayley added that the new Royal Beach Club "already become the #1 top-rated experience in the Nassau for our cruise guests."
  • Elizabeth Dove, Goldman Sachs: Asked about yield cadence for the year. Holtz explained that dry dock timing and the ramp-up of Royal Beach Club influence yield patterns, with larger ships in dry dock impacting comparisons.
  • Robin Farley, UBS: Asked about the Discovery class ships and cost sustainability. Bayley said Discovery "really is going to be a game changer," while Holtz emphasized cost discipline, leveraging scale and AI for sustainable efficiencies.
  • Brandt Montour, Barclays: Queried about industry rationality in pricing. Liberty observed that "this industry is so much more rational, so much more about price integrity."
  • Conor Cunningham, Melius Research: Asked about short-duration itineraries and close-in demand. Liberty described a mature state for short products and noted AI-driven yield management is improving forecasting and pricing.
  • David Katz, Jefferies: Requested insights into River Cruise expansion. Liberty stated, "this is not a hobby for us," and demand from loyalists has been strong, with 80% of bookings from existing customers new to river cruising, according to Holtz.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive yet probing tone, focusing on yield sustainability, Caribbean capacity, and strategic expansion initiatives.
  • Management consistently used confident language, highlighting strong consumer demand, technology advantages, and innovation. Liberty reiterated, "we feel very passionate that we'll continue to innovate and that sets us very well to continue to win that share from that $2 trillion."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence remained high, with analysts slightly more focused on potential headwinds in Caribbean pricing and cost controls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance is more specific, with an explicit EPS target of $17.70 to $18.10 for 2026, up from the previous quarter's "$17 handle." Revenue growth expectations shifted from "nearly $6 billion operating cash flow" to "over $7 billion" for 2026.
  • Strategic focus intensified on river cruise expansion and the launch of the Discovery class ships. The previous quarter highlighted Perfect Day Mexico and Royal Beach Club Santorini; this quarter expands to 10 more river vessels and Discovery class investments.
  • Analysts' focus has shifted from general demand and yield trends to more granular concerns about Caribbean capacity, yield cadence, and cost management sustainability.
  • Management's tone remained confident, with increased emphasis on technological investments and loyalty program enhancements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged challenges related to dry dock timing and deployment mix, particularly impacting yield growth in the first half of 2026.
  • Cost headwinds are expected from the ramp-up of private destination operations and increased exposure to EU ETS compliance for European itineraries.
  • Analysts raised concerns about Caribbean capacity increases and the potential impact on close-in pricing, as well as questions about long-term cost discipline.

Final Takeaway

Royal Caribbean Cruises delivered record financial and operational results in 2025, fueled by strong demand, innovation, and disciplined execution. The company announced significant strategic expansions in river cruising and new ship classes, while providing concrete guidance for double-digit revenue growth and an adjusted EPS range of $17.70 to $18.10 in 2026. Management emphasized robust booking trends, ongoing cost efficiencies, and continued investments in technology and loyalty programs, positioning the company for sustained growth and shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Barrick Mining initiated at Buy, Agnico Eagle at Neutral by J.P. Morgan
data.description:
J.P. Morgan launched coverage of the North American gold sector with an Overweight rating and $68 price target for Barrick Mining (B) and a Neutral rating with a $248 PT for Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) in the context of both a near- and long-term bullish outlook for gold, supported by central bank buying and U.S. policy uncertainty.

JPM's Bennett Moore said he views Agnico Eagle (AEM) as the premier player in the space, underpinned by its operational excellence, attractive cost profile, and low-risk regional focus, which comes at a premium, but with the next leg of growth primarily a 2030s story and current valuation relatively full, the analyst said he would wait for a better entry point.

On the other hand, Barrick (B) offers a world-class reserve base and compelling organic growth nearer term, but with a mixed track record, ongoing management transition and exposure to risky jurisdictions, and its shares trade at a greater discount to global peers vs. historical levels but with potential idiosyncratic catalysts on the horizon, including a Loulo-Gounkoto restart or sale and a Fourmile resource update.

Barrick (B) and Agnico Eagle (AEM) rose earlier Thursday to multiyear highs of $54.69 and $225, respectively, but have since pulled back, now -2.4% and -4.6% as gold futures retreated from a record above $5,500/oz.
